text: Everything else was quiet. Ronin kept his advance slow, sweeping his optics from side to side, boots silent on the concrete floor. The small rooms along the hall had been mostly pilfered, the desks, chairs, and shelves inside battered and broken like most everything else in this world. One of the doors, removed from its hinges, lay in the hallway. That was different . He paused before crossing the open doorway, easing himself against the wall. The coating of dust was undisturbed, save for where it had been kicked up by the door falling, but that didn’t mean the place was empty. There were many, bots and humans alike, who would favor a place like this over the lights of Cheyenne . Numbers and walls did not always mean security . Leaning forward slightly, he peered past the jamb. His processors rapidly compared the room to the last reference in his memory. Nothing had changed, save for the fallen door. Satisfied, Ronin continued down the hall . Reaching the end, he let himself into the large room that had once served as a place for humans to congregate and eat. The long tables and chairs were overturned, legs broken off, and sunlight streamed in through the shattered windows. Weapon raised, he scanned the vicinity . Though the dust here was constantly shifted by the wind, there were boot prints clearly visible throughout. They wove between the tables and into the dark kitchen, and along the wall to the right, stopping at the restroom. The gait was uneven, the tread obscured in places due to the left foot dragging. Either a human or a damaged bot, though Ronin suspected even a damaged bot would have more regularity in its stride . He side-stepped to the counter, adjusting to night-vision to scan the kitchen. Someone had been through recently and torn it apart, as though there’d really be any food left after so long; by human count, the Black Out had been at least fifteen years before Ronin was woken by the Prophet, and Ronin was now in his one hundred and eighty-fifth year since reactivation. People had trouble preserving their crops for a few months , these days . Ronin crossed the cafeteria, following the tracks to the restrooms. He stopped at the door with the faded image of a circle on the top point of a triangle. It was the female restroom, though he didn’t understand why that data had survived the Black Out when so much else was lost. Keeping the stock of his rifle firm against his shoulder, he slowly pushed the door open with his left hand. Its rusty hinges whined in protest . There was no light inside; he switched his optics to infrared and swept the room. The stalls were all open, all empty, and the only heat he detected was from his own reflection in the shattered mirror — brightest where it seeped through the bullet holes in his casing . He moved directly to the men’s room. It was similarly cold; whoever had been here was gone. Reverting to his normal optics, Ronin drew his flashlight from an inside coat pocket and clicked it on . The remains of a small fire lay beneath the open ceiling vent, the wood charred black and crumbling. Nearby were a few tiny scraps of cloth, the dried-out bones of a small animal — a prairie dog, most likely — and a crumpled tin can. The dust wasn’t as thick in this room, but the disturbed spot on the ground indicated someone had lain near the fire . Ronin stepped carefully around the leavings and moved to the sink. The porcelain was yellowed and run through with fine cracks, but remained, surprisingly, in one piece. Within lay a few hand-rolled cigarettes, flakes of whatever plant had been used to make them gathered in the basin. A canteen stood on the flat part of the sink, nearest the mirror, wrapped in tattered canvas . Dark, rust-colored drops had dried around the edges and inside of the sink and soaked into some of the paper. Blood . An injured human had sheltered here, and either left or was taken in a hurry . Letting the shoulder strap take the weight of the rifle, Ronin picked up the canteen and shook it. Liquid sloshed inside. It didn’t have many uses for a bot, but it would for a human. He’d done good trade with them in the past. If not in Cheyenne, he’d trade it elsewhere. There were other towns on the edge of the Dust, and nothing truly tied him to Cheyenne . He slipped the container into a coat pocket. It clinked softly against the ammunition. Returning to the cafeteria, he turned the flashlight off and put it away, turning his optics to the sunlight. It had taken on the red-orange hue that signaled coming sunset . There were thousands more square feet to comb over, even without the trailers outside, but it would be best to head back. Minor as it was, his damage would only cause more complications the longer he ignored it, and his pack was full of scrap already . He’d spent 51,642 nights in the Dust and surrounding wilds since he was awoken. More than enough time to know that, despite night-vision and infrared optics, the world was even more dangerous after dark . Return, trade, recharge, repair. Then he could come back out again . * * * T he Bot District’s lights shone bright as Ronin closed in on Cheyenne’s eastern entryway. The buildings inside the wall stood in stark contrast to the ones without; most of the structures in the Bot District had been constructed before the Black Out, maintained over the years by automated units that had such tasks hard-coded in their programming. Many such bots existed in many towns. The majority lacked the higher cognitive functions of more advanced bots and synths . Still, Ronin envied them —
